data.title: XSMO: Compelling Value And Growth Mix Persists For These Small-Cap Momentum Stocks
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last reviewed the Invesco S&P SmallCap Momentum ETF (XSMO) on July 15, 2025, when I rated it a "buy" on account of its outstanding GARP features and relatively high quality. Since that article was published, XSMO has gained 17.41%, outpacing XMMO and SPMO, its mid- and large-cap counterparts, by 2.37% and 11.13%, respectively.

The purpose of this article is to provide an update on XSMO's fundamentals to ensure the advantages from July remain, which isn't guaranteed, given its index's semi-annual reconstitution schedule. Fortunately, I'm still satisfied XSMO is positioned quite well from a GARP perspective, given its 20.91% estimated earnings growth rate and 18.36x forward P/E ratio, so barring any significant changes next month, I'm confident in reiterating my "buy" rating.

XSMO Overview

XSMO has a 0.36% expense ratio and $2.4B in assets under management and launched on March 3, 2005, making it a fairly well-established fund in the small-cap space. However, it's only tracked its current index, the S&P SmallCap Momentum Index, since June 21, 2019. That makes long-term performance analysis challenging, but I'll try to make up for it with a detailed fundamental analysis that, in my opinion, is far more useful.

The Index itself is quite easy to understand. The selection universe is the S&P SmallCap 600 Index, and the Index provider calculates momentum scores based on each security's one-year price return (excluding the most recent month). The top quintile (120 securities) with the highest momentum scores are selected, with weights determined based on the product of the security's momentum score and market cap, subject to the following constraint:

Lastly, the Index rebalances after the close on the third Friday of March and September, meaning a change is coming in about a month or so. Historically, XSMO's annual portfolio turnover rates have been high, ranging from 107% to 147% over the last five years, per its latest annual financial statements. Therefore, it's reasonable to expect some changes, but I'm counting on the fund remaining fundamentally strong - that is, the changes will result in a portfolio that still has good growth, value, quality, and, of course, momentum characteristics.

XSMO Performance Analysis

A $10,000 investment in XSMO on July 1, 2019, would have resulted in a balance of $22,463 on February 18, 2016, which translates to a 124.63% total return, or 12.91% annualized. That's a solid gain but well behind the 15.67% and 18.95% figures for XMMO and SPMO. XSMO was hurt early on by its poor performance in the Q1 2020 COVID-19 crash, where it dropped by 25.53%. It also failed to keep up with SPMO in recent years as the large-cap momentum Index often held AI-leading stocks like Nvidia (NVDA).

High downside risk is something to watch out for with XSMO and many small-cap ETFs, as small-cap stocks are typically more volatile than large-cap ones. We see from the performance statistics table above how XSMO's standard deviation was the highest (21.49%) and its risk-adjusted returns, as measured by the Sharpe and Sortino ratios, were the lowest. To make matters worse, XSMO's best and worst three-month returns were also relatively poor, so over this 6.5-year period, there's not a lot of good news to report.

However, at the risk of sounding too optimistic, this time might be different. In the calendar year returns table below, we see that XSMO is leading in 2026 with a 10.72% return, and it hasn't experienced a single-month loss of more than 5% in a year (February 2025). Meanwhile, it's had three +5% months in the last year compared to just two each for XMMO and SPMO, so for me, this demonstrates consistent outperformance rather than any one-off gains that are too difficult to predict in advance.

XSMO Fundamental Analysis

Supporting XSMO's strong recent returns are compelling fundamentals, which I've summarized in the table below. It includes statistics that cover all the major factors (size, risk, growth, value, quality, and momentum), but it's the balance that impresses me the most. Simply put, XSMO has few weak spots, and if you treat investing as a game of probabilities, the odds are in XSMO's favor.

The first thing to highlight is that XSMO's the best-diversified of the three, evidenced by its 41.05% concentration in its top 20 holdings compared to 55.42% and 67.80% for XMMO and SPMO. Its 1.11 five-year portfolio beta implies lower market risk than SPMO, which reduces some of the volatility concerns I observed earlier. Also, XSMO's quality features are actually better than XMMO's, which is rare to see in a small-cap fund. For example, XSMO holds the edge on EBIT margins (16.08% vs. 14.57%), net margins (13.89%), and return on total capital (9.52% vs. 7.48%), so despite higher historical drawdowns and worse risk-adjusted returns, I view XSMO as the safer option right now based on its current composition.

Good risk and quality features help on the downside, but XSMO also has excellent growth features, evidenced by its components' 20.49% three-year EPS CAGR and 20.91% one-year estimated EPS growth rate. That's slightly better than XMMO and a few percentage points behind SPMO, but the key difference is on valuation. XSMO's 18.36x forward P/E ratio represents a 17% and 22% discount to XMMO and SPMO, while its discount using its 18.95x trailing P/E ratio is even more substantial.

I worry that XMMO's and SPMO's high trailing P/Es will compress as earnings surprises start to decelerate, and with most companies reporting this quarter, that's exactly what's happening. Overall, the weighted average surprise for S&P 500 Index stocks this quarter is 5.61%, which is similar to the 5.49% figure noted for SPMO in the table. Meanwhile, XSMO's figure is 11.86%, so at least for now, earnings surprises are correlating well with price returns, and that's something that's consistent with my overall investment philosophy because it demonstrates that the market is at least somewhat efficient.

Investment Recommendation

XSMO's strong performance since my last review is supported by compelling fundamentals, particularly its better-than-expected quality and an excellent combination of growth and value. Notably, XSMO's Index only screens for one-year price momentum, so producing a portfolio that trades at just 18.36x forward earnings while offering 20%+ historical and estimated earnings per share growth is impressive. These are the reasons I've decided to maintain my "buy" rating, but the only word of caution I have for readers is to monitor its semi-annual reconstitutions, as they could materially change the fund's factor mix. I don't anticipate that happening next month, but regardless, I plan to provide another update at that time. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Kymera Therapeutics: The More This Biotech Spends, The Higher Its Valuation Rises
data.description:
Investment Overview

Kymera Therapeutics (KYMR) is a Watertown, Massachusetts based biotech I have been covering since I shared a note with Seeking Alpha back in January 2021, issuing a Buy rating.

Just over five years on, the stock trades ~8% higher than it did at the time that note was published. Its share price at the time of writing is $87.5, and the market cap valuation is $6.91bn.

The stock is up >100% on a six-month basis, >130% on a 12-month basis, and up >330% since its 2020 Initial Public Offering ("IPO") raised ~$174m at $20 per share.

Kymera's specialty has always been protein degradation, albeit originally in the oncology field, and the company had racked up an accumulated deficit of ~$1bn by the of Q3 2025, before announcing another fundraising of $620m in December last year.

As we can see below, this is a company whose cash burn has increased in every year since listing:

Kymera - Pushing Candidate KT-621 As Long-Term Dupixent Replacement

An overview of where the company is at today in terms of its pipeline is shared below (source: Q3 earnings presentation).

To have raised ~$1.5bn in funding, and only made it to the Phase 2b clinical study stage with a single asset may not feel like much of a return, but the company's target immunology markets (shown in the slide below) are so vast that a best-in-class product is arguably worth waiting for, and Kymera's technology and targets are somewhat unique.

The fact that Kymera's lead candidate is orally available is promising, in markets dominated by injectable therapies, and the company clearly believes it has "pipeline in a pill" potential.

Speaking at the JPM Healthcare conference last month, Kymera's CEO Nello Mainolfi told the audience, amongst other things:

this year, in May will be the tenth year anniversary since we founded Kymera and we spent the past 10 years building strategy capabilities and team to deliver a whole new generation of medicines. What we're trying to build is something that has actually never been done before: oral drugs with biologics-like activity.

About 2 years ago, around this conference, we presented, for the first time, our STAT6 program. We showed with preclinical studies that if you degrade STAT6, you can deliver activity, the blockade of IL-4 and 13 in cells and in vivo models that can mimic if not being numerically superior to what has been seen with a mega blockbuster drug like dupilumab.

We continue to collaborate with Sanofi (SNY) on our IRAK4 degrader and decided to advance the second-generation degrader in the clinic this year. We entered the collaboration with Gilead on this novel first-in-class CDK2 molecular glue for oncology. And we were able to raise almost $1 billion last year, which gives us about now $1.6 billion that will fund the company through at least into 2029.

The reason why degraders can accomplish this is because one molecule of our degrader can degrade hundreds, if not thousands of cabrio protein. We can only fit 5 on this slide. But believe me, we can do more than that. And so this allows us to have a catalytic mechanism. So a small amount of drug for a short amount of time, can degrade the protein completely and fully.

Plenty of positives to consider, in my view, but can a charismatic management team convert a promise into something more tangible, within the next couple of years?

As we can see above (slide taken from the JPM Healthcare conference presentation), critical Phase 2b STAT6 data is not due until 2027, and KT-579's Phase 1 study has apparently not yet begun, so unfortunately, more patience may be required before a commercial approval and a revenue stream can be contemplated.

CEO Manioli sums up 2026 as follows:

So 2026, a busy year. The goal is to complete enrollment of the AD study with KT-621, continue to enroll the asthma study to generate data by '27, initiate and complete and release the data for the Phase 1 study of KT-579. As I just mentioned earlier, we hopefully will be in the position to disclose a new program in immunology that will be going into IND-enabling studies later this year. And then, again, continue to advance our partner program to important inflection points.

The CEO claims that KT-621 could supplant dupilimab - French Pharma Sanofi (SNY) and US Pharma Regeneron's (REGN) co-developed Dupixent - as a future standard of care in double-digit billion dollar immunology markets, and although the clock must surely now be ticking, Kymera arguably remains the most promising protein degrader to monitor in this space.

Most other listed protein degrader specialists - Zentalis Pharma (ZNTL), C4 Therapeutics (CCCC), Olema Pharmaceuticals (OLMA), Arvinas (ARVN), Foghorn Therapeutics (FHTX), for example, are focused on oncology, as Kymera originally was, although Monte Rosa Therapeutics (GLUE) is also worth keeping an eye on in the immunology space.

Given that STAT6 is the "specific transcription factor in the IL-4/IL-13 pathway", and that Dupixent is an interleukin-4 ("IL-4") receptor alpha antagonist, in some ways the two drugs' mechanisms of action ("MoA") are not so very different, but Kymera says that some ~55% of adults with moderate / severe atopic dermatitis ("AD") and 60% of asthma patients report inadequate disease control, therefore despite the runaway success of Dupixent, which earned >$17.5bn of revenues in 2025, up ~25% year-on-year, there is room for improvement.

Preclinical data shared by Kymera shows that KT-621 may inhibit certain disease biomarkers, such as TARC (inflammation suppression), Eotaxin-3 (chemotaxis), and IgE (mast cell activation) as effectively or slightly more effectively than dupilimab, although frankly speaking, it is usually unwise to invest in a company based on its preclinical data - real clinical data - the more the better - is the only true measure of efficacy / safety.

In December last year, Kymera did share some Phase 1b data AD data for KT-621 - the company noted in a press release that:

  • Robust clinical activity was observed across all disease endpoints measured including mean 63% EASI reduction and mean 40% peak pruritus NRS reduction for all patients
  • Patients with comorbid asthma showed a median 56% reduction in FeNO and meaningful improvements in asthma control, and those with comorbid allergic rhinitis experienced significant symptom and quality-of-life benefits

CEO Mainolfi was quoted as follows:

The results were in line with, or in some cases numerically exceeded, published data for dupilumab at week 4 and we believe further reinforce Kymera’s pioneering expertise in developing transformative oral small molecules with the potential for the activity and safety of injectable biologics.

Dupixent secured approval to treat adolescents with AD based on a study showing:

The average improvement in the Eczema Area and Severity Index (EASI) from baseline was approximately 66% compared to 24% for placebo

The news triggered a spike in Kymera's share price from ~$67, to $94, which, for a Phase 1b readout, is a significant hike. Management says the aim of the Phase 2b study is to establish clinical activity and safety, select an appropriate dose for a Phase 3 study, and to "support registrational studies in multiple dermatological and gastrointestinal indications".

Looking Ahead - Kymera Tests Investor's Patience, But Prize May Be Worth Waiting For

Kymera is far from the only company working on the "next Dupixent" - in fact, Sanofi is partnering with biotech Recludix on a:

potent and selective oral STAT6 inhibitor that has shown complete 100% pathway inhibition and potent efficacy in preclinical models of asthma, acute lung inflammation and dermatitis

Sanofi walked away from a partnership with Kymera over an IRAK4 degrader candidate last year (I covered this in a note for SA at the time) however Kymera notes in its Q3 2025 10Q filing:

In June 2025, Sanofi communicated its decision to exercise its full participation election under the terms of the companies’ collaboration agreement, and to advance KT-485/SAR447971, into clinical testing.

As a result, Sanofi intends to stop development of KT-474. In connection with the IRAK4 program, the Company remains eligible to receive up to $975 million in development and commercial milestones upon the achievement of certain developmental or regulatory events and upon the achievement of certain net sales thresholds.

Meanwhile, the collaboration with Gilead Sciences (GILD), announced at almost the same time as the Sanofi disappointment, involves $750m in milestones, and tiered royalties on future net sales running into the high teen percentages. Despite pledging itself to be an immunology only company only a few weeks before this deal was made, this collaboration is in fact focused on developing oncology drugs.

It may be hard to escape the view that Kymera is testing investor's patience to the limit - for example, when I covered the company in March 2024, it was heading into a Phase 2 study with KT-474, and was advancing two Phase 1 stage oncology drugs, yet today, so far as I am aware, all three programs have been shelved.

Bearing this in mind, it may seem inexplicable that Kymera's share price is in fact up >115% since that note, based on not much more that a small Phase 1b study, or that the company has been able to raise a significant amount of funding.

In my view, there are two ways to look at this - Kymera is a money pit that is best avoided, or, Kymera is on to something, and when the CEO says it has taken ten years to prepare the ground for a truly successful protein degrader candidate, he should be taken at face value.

I am not sure I have come across a company that has raised so much cash without producing so much as a promising set of Phase 2 data, but with that said, the concept of protein degradation has always intrigued me - marking disease causing proteins for destruction by the immune system - therefore if I were a shareholder in Kymera, I suspect I would stay the course.

Would I buy stock today? Clearly, if Kymera truly has a next-generation Dupixent in its pipeline - an oral "pipeline in a pill", then its valuation has so much more upside to explore.

I'd compare it to a company like Alnylam (ALNY), for example, which for a decade post-listing struggled to make its RNA-interference technology work, but is now a >$45bn market cap Pharma with multiple approved products, including Amvuttra, a drug with ~$9bn per annum peak revenue potential, analysts believe.

Personally, I cannot give Kymera a Buy Rating today because its pipeline still has not taken meaningful shape, and I suspect the odds of KT-621 being the next Dupixent are remote, with so many clinical and regulatory hurdles still to overcome.

Nevertheless, I would urge readers to keep an eye on this company - I don't think I have ever witnessed such a strong correlation between increasing cash burn and increasing share price before with a biotech company, which is not an endorsement, but if Kymera has been meticulously preparing the ground for its lead asset for a decade, as the CEO has suggested, successfully winning over the institutional investment community in the process, then perhaps, in 2027, the Phase 2b readout will be a genuine "mic drop" moment.

data.title: GDP Prints At Feeble 1.4% On Shutdown As Trump Counters On SCOTUS Tariff Decision
data.description:
NEW YORK (February 20th) - Gross Domestic Product, or GDP, for the Fourth Quarter of 2025 (2025 Q4) printed this morning at just 1.4%, well below the consensus estimate of 3.5%. Real final sales to private domestic purchasers, the sum of consumer spending and gross private fixed investment, increased 2.4%.

The print was even below the lower bound of our 2.25% estimate of 2025 Q4 in our January public jobs report.* The "Blue Chip" consensus tracked by the St. Louis Fed had a wide range of estimates, but the Atlanta Fed’s near real-time GDP Now tracker, which monitors a number of data releases to regularly revise its estimate, printed at 3%. Let's look at our exclusive chart of 2025 Q2 GDP by Major Component:

The obvious anomaly in GDP is Government Consumption Expenditures, which declined 90 bps and 115 bps at the federal level, as detailed here:

The other components of GDP are largely unremarkable, except Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) are just about half what they were in 2024 Q4.

President Trump, who is typically briefed on the GDP data before it is released, posted on his Truth Social account early this morning, about 40 minutes before its official release:

ANALYSIS & PROGNOSTICATION

We have to agree with President Trump that Fourth Quarter GDP was affected by the government shutdown for 43 days commencing the first day of the quarter, so half of the 90-day period. Given the size of the government workforce, the shutdown may also have weakened the Personal Consumption Expenditures ("PCE") data, too.

The current limited shutdown may affect some spending in the GCE and PCE sectors, too, but not as materially as 2025 Q4.

The effects of today’s decision by the Supreme Court, striking down President Trump’s International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) tariffs, remain to be seen. But it would likely negatively affect Net Exports, or NEX, because there will be more imports since tariffs were struck down. It would also negatively affect Gross Domestic Private Investment, or GDPI, as companies that had planned to build factories and produce in the USA would have canceled such plans. However, it appears President Trump has taken up Justice Kavanaugh's suggestion to impose tariffs under an authority other than the IEEPA. He wrote in his dissent from the majority decision,

Although I firmly disagree with the Court’s holding today, the decision might not substantially constrain a President’s ability to order tariffs going forward. That is because numerous other federal statutes authorize the President to impose tariffs and might justify most (if not all) of the tariffs at issue in this case—albeit perhaps with a few additional procedural steps that IEEPA, as an emergency statute, does not require. Those statutes include, for example, the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 (Section 232); the Trade Act of 1974 (Sections 122, 201, and301); and the Tariff Act of 1930 (Section 338). In essence, the Court today concludes that the President checked the wrong statutory box by relying on IEEPA rather than another statute to impose these tariffs.

Those other statutes allow President Trump to circumvent today’s decision and re-impose his tariffs almost immediately under those other statutes. It appears, as of this writing, that this White House will do that, though it's not clear the move will survive another round of judicial scrutiny. Briefs are likely being written right now to obtain temporary restraining orders to prohibit the White House from reimposing tariffs. Given the negative effect on NEX and GDI and the uncertainty as to whether the White House imposition of tariffs under rules suggested in Justice Kavanaugh's dissent, we’re reducing our estimate of 2026Q1 GDP to 1.75%, +/- 50 bps.

For timely updates of our view on this and other matters, please follow us on our X (Twitter) feed, @stuysquare or our pages on LinkedIn and Facebook. We usually submit our jobs analysis for publication the same day that BLS releases the data.

* We had been more accurate in our December jobs report, which put 2025 Q4 GDP at 1.5%, +/- 25 bps. However, we revised that estimate higher based on January hiring, which indicated to us that the fourth quarter must have been stronger than we estimated. That revision and assessment proved to be in error, at least for this preliminary estimate.

Note: Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe to be underperforming and include strategies that we would recommend for the companies of our clients. Others discuss new management strategies we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions here regarding whether to buy, sell, or hold such companies, however, assume the company will not change its current practices).

data.title: A U.S.-Iran War Could Trigger A Global Recession
data.description:
High volatility expected

The S&P 500 (SP500) is just below the all-time highs, and yet the VIX (VIX) is near 20. This means investors expect high volatility over the near term - and this could mean a deep correction but also a spike to the new all-time highs.

The VIX is near 20 because investors "fear" several imminent and unfolding risk events.

  • First, a potential AI bubble burst is unfolding, with the recent selloff in software stocks (IGV) and also financials (XLF) deemed to be vulnerable to AI disruption, along with general underperformance of the Mag7 (MAGS).
  • Second, the SCOTUS just ruled against IEEPA tariffs, with the entire Trump economic plan at stake.
  • Third, currently it appears that a US attack on Iran is imminent, and under a certain scenario it could produce a spike in the price of oil (USO) and possibly trigger a global recession.

Any of these three risk events by itself could trigger a massive selloff in the stock market if materialized. The problem is that all three risk events could materialize at the same time. Thus, the VIX is high, near 20, and if any of these risks materialize, the VIX will likely spike to above 40 as the stock market sells off.

Obviously, if these risks don't materialize 1) the AI disruption fears abate, 2) the SCOTUS decision on tariffs does not affect the economy negatively, and 3) the conflict with Iran does not produce a spike in oil; the VIX would fall and the stock market would rally in relief, with the S&P 500 (SPY) breaking the 7000 level to the new all-time highs.

The US-Iran situation: macro context

The US has been building a large military position in the Gulf, and it seems like the attack on Iran is imminent. The major risk for investors is the spike in the price of oil if the attack does happen.

Note, the US already attacked Iran in 2025, and the price of oil did not spike. The 2025 attack on Iran was in the context of the Israel-Iran 12-day war, which was in the context of Israel's war against Hamas and other Iranian proxies in the region in response to the October 7th, 2023, attack on Israel.

Israel had a legitimate concern that Iran was close to developing a nuclear weapon, and only the US had the capability to destroy Iran's nuclear capabilities with B2 bombers.

The US successfully destroyed key Iranian nuclear capabilities, but it seems like Iran is rebuilding its nuclear program. Thus, the pretense of the current situation is Iran's nuclear program - the US wants Iran to completely stop all nuclear activities, along with the long-range ballistic missiles program and the support for regional terrorist groups.

It appears that the negotiations with Iran have failed, and thus the US will have to attack Iran again.

The broader objectives

However, the current situation with Iran is more complex, and it's part of the broader trend of global fragmentation and specifically the US-China relationship.

Specifically, we are in the process of de-globalization and fragmentation into two blocks - the US-led block and the China-led block (and possibly even a third EU-led block).

The US-China decoupling is facing a major obstacle - China controls most of the global supply and manufacturing of rare earths. In October of 2025, China enacted an embargo on rare earths - this this was a major problem for the US, given that rare earths are essential for military purposes.

After negotiations, China agreed to delay the rare-earths export ban for a year, until Nov 2026. Since the US has been in a global search for alternative sources of rare earths, which included the episode with Greenland.

China has leverage over rare earths, while the US is trying to get leverage over global oil supplies. Note, China imports most of its oil; energy security is a major problem for China.

Venezuela exports most of its oil to China, and the US now controls the Venezuelan oil exports.

Iran also exports most of its oil to China through the Strait of Hormuz. Thus, by taking control of Iran's oil exports, the US would gain leverage over China.

At that point the only major source of oil for China would be Russia - and Russia is still at war with Ukraine. Thus, Russian oil exports to China could also be vulnerable.

Thus, it seems like the US objective is to gain leverage over China in oil supplies to counter China's leverage in rare earths. Iran is part of the plan. Thus, a US attack on Iran is very likely, and it will require regime change in Iran.

Implications for investors

The current situation with Iran is more complex than the 2025 situation. It seems like the objectives are the regime change and the control of Iran's oil exports.

Iran's regime is likely to be more desperate in its efforts to survive, and thus, we can expect more desperate measures like the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and possible attacks on the regional energy infrastructure.

Thus, the spike in oil is more likely in the current situation.

If the conflict is prolonged, and it escalates to a regional war, we are facing a scenario of a prolonged spike in oil above $100/barrel - and this could trigger a global recession.

Thus, investors should evaluate primarily the duration of the conflict and who controls the Strait of Hormuz. Oil is already rising, currently at $66/barrel for WTI, and it's likely that it will spike as the conflict starts.

If it appears that the US will attack only Iran's nuclear capabilities, without the objective of regime change - it will likely be a short-term spike, with the opportunity to short oil and buy the dip in stocks as the VIX drops.

However, if it becomes obvious that the objective is regime change to control Iran's oil exports, investors should brace for a more prolonged oil price spike, which could cause a global recession.

data.title: Western Union: Attractive 10% Yield From A Remittance Giant Going Digital
data.description:
Intro

Hunting for large‑cap, high‑yield dividend plays led me to The Western Union Company (WU) last October. The yield was an eye‑catching 11% back then, and I was quite sure I’d uncover something beneath the surface that explained why such a high payout couldn’t possibly be sustainable.

From what I’ve seen, yields above 7% usually hint at underlying issues or some kind of special situation. There’s no free lunch, after all. But the deeper I dug, the more it became clear that WU case deserves a different lens — it might even be one of those rare occasions where the exception genuinely proves the rule.

Since I broke down Western Union’s longer-term journey in more detail in my last piece, let’s move straight to the freshest development — today’s earnings release.

Q4 FY2025 Earnings

Adjusted Q4 revenue came in at $1.0 billion, down 5% YOY, missing expectations by roughly $30 million, while full‑year adjusted revenue reached $4.1 billion, down 2% excluding Iraq impact ($ transfers in Iraq were suspended in late 2023 by the Iraqi Central Bank, but were gradually restored during 2024). Consumer Services remained the highest growth segment, with adjusted revenue up 26% in Q4 and 29% for the full year. Branded Digital delivered a steady 6% adjusted growth in both periods. Q4 adjusted EPS of $0.45 beat estimates by $0.02, bringing full‑year adjusted EPS to $1.75.

The company attributed the revenue miss to weakness in its Americas retail business. Still, the good news was that after a dismal November, the U.S.–Mexico corridor stabilized in December:

The CEO expressed optimism in the earnings release regarding the company’s overall trajectory:

“We strengthened our Consumer Services offerings, expanded our owned retail footprint, and accelerated our transition to a more digital-first operating model. Looking ahead to 2026, we are confident in our ability to execute against our Beyond strategy as we expand our capabilities, drive operating efficiencies, and position the company for sustainable long-term growth.”

Despite the CEO’s positive tone, the adjusted revenue trend still doesn’t look good, but adjusted margins can be viewed as performing well:

The next slide provides a great illustration of the challenging trends caused by the U.S. administration’s tight immigration policies:

Coming into 2026, the CEO sees encouraging signs in the Mexico corridor:

The Bank of Mexico data would seem to indicate that the worst may be behind us, with principal growth in the most recent reported month going slightly positive on a year-over-year basis, improving materially from the summer lows.

Dividend Safety and Stock Buybacks

Given the current attractive dividend yield of around 10%, I think it’s important to assess the sustainability of the dividend.

The dividend was not addressed on the earnings call; the likely reason, in my view, is that there are no concerns, given the company’s strong cash generation, solid balance sheet, and diversified business model.

While the company’s free cash flow has in past years comfortably covered dividend payouts and still allowed for meaningful share repurchases, the CFO noted that buybacks will be reduced to about $100 million in 2026, as the Intermax closing and integration will require additional capital.

All financial metrics remain solid, and the company maintains investment‑grade ratings, indicating no current risk to the dividend. Moreover, FY2025 free cash flow would have been even stronger — slightly above $600 million — as the reported figure includes $220 million in cash taxes paid related to the transition tax, as the CFO highlighted on the earnings call.

Outlook

While the midpoint of the adjusted EPS outlook came in just $0.01 above the $1.79 market expected, the revenue outlook is meaningfully stronger, as the 6–9% growth range translates into a midpoint of $4.33 billion versus the $4.12 billion consensus. That said, as the market’s reaction also reflects — with the stock declining rather than advancing — the outlook is not directly comparable, given that the company expects to close the Intermex takeover and begin consolidating it starting in Q3 2026.

The CFO provided the following insights regarding the recent Intermex transaction and the impact of higher interest costs:

Our adjusted revenue outlook for 2026 is for 6%-9% revenue growth, inclusive of Intermex, which we now expect to close in the second quarter of this year. Our adjusted EPS for the full year, we believe, will be between $1.75 to $1.85. This includes higher interest expense as we look to refinance our existing notes coming to maturity in Q1, which are currently yielding 1.35% to something closer to today’s interest rate environment.

…and later offered clearer context around the messy revenue guide, noting that organic growth is expected to be flattish — excluding Intermex —which explains the stock’s sell‑off, as it slightly misses the 2026 revenue estimates.

..organic’s going to be closer to flattish, and then Intermex will help us get closer to the 6%-7%. We think we’ll get some positive growth, but call it flattish.

Management expects continued strong growth in consumer services:

We expect consumer services to have another strong year in 2026, as our travel money business is expected to approach $150 million in revenue, up from nearly nothing a few years ago.

Key Growth Initiatives

Within the broader digital strategy, WU management is placing a strong focus on the digital wallet, and the early results are very encouraging:

Our goal is to create a two-sided network that makes it easy for our customers to move funds cross-border while staying within the Western Union ecosystem. In addition to our U.S. Wallet, we are continuing to see strong results out of our wallets in both Argentina and Brazil.

In Brazil now, we have onboarded roughly 20,000 customers since our launch in May of last year, and in the most recent month, we have redirected roughly 5% of all inbound transfers to the country into our wallet. This saves commission expense, as well as gives us an opportunity to increase retention and potentially monetize our receiver base

The slide below illustrates their global wallet initiatives in my view very effectively:

The opportunities within their highly promising stablecoin platform are summarized on the next slide. It is currently in the testing phase, but is expected to go live within the next couple of quarters.

The CEO provided some valuable remarks regarding the latest stablecoin/USDPT dynamics:

...we have successfully minted our first U.S. dollar payment token, USDPT, and have moved it between our Treasury Department and our agents’ wallets.

These pilots are focused on leveraging on-chain settlement to reduce dependency on legacy correspondent banking systems, shorten settlement windows, and improve our capital efficiency. We see significant opportunities for us to move money faster and at lower cost, without compromising compliance or customer trust. These milestones put us on a path to meet our expectation of offering our payment token to the market by the middle of this year. I’m happy to report that we remain on schedule, and we were looking forward to our market launch. Finally, we are expanding our partnerships and capabilities to allow our customers to move and hold stablecoin digital assets and to allow them to have more control in how they manage and move their money. In many parts of the world, being able to hold a U.S. dollar-denominated asset has real value as inflation and currency devaluation rapidly erodes an individual’s purchasing power.

In addition to the exciting digital developments, management also highlighted several significant wins in onboarding large‑scale partnerships on the legacy side of the business. Deutsche Post is an exceptional win, as their paths had diverged just a few years ago, but they ultimately concluded that WU offers the best value in this segment.

Risks

As the CEO highlighted on the earnings call, political risk is currently elevated across the globe and introduces a high degree of uncertainty:

..any given day, an election or a geopolitical change can happen that disrupts the region again for another 3-6 months.

..the landscape for human capital mobility continues to shift each and every day. For example, in the last quarter, we saw an election in Chile that will have implications across the region for immigration and mobility. Also, a change in leadership in Venezuela, for which the implications are still being assessed.

Additional key risks include macro uncertainty, ongoing U.S. immigration policy headwinds, potential regulatory changes, and execution challenges as the company transitions from a legacy remittance model to a more modern fintech platform.

Overall, in my view, the business remains well‑diversified across markets, and its scale and strong financial position provide a meaningful buffer against these risks.

Valuation

In my view, WU remains clearly undervalued across virtually all valuation metrics, and I fully align with the SA A‑grade.

To add further context, I calculated a forward‑looking EV/FCF multiple and arrived at 7.8x. The calculation is based on the midpoint of the company’s estimated $570 million free cash flow outlook provided at the Investor Day in November 2025, and a current Enterprise Value of approximately $4.5 billion (319 million shares at $9, market cap of $2.9 billion, cash of $1.3 billion, and debt of $2.9 billion).

Taking all of this into account, and considering the company’s broader business prospects, I maintain my Buy rating.

Takeaway

While WU’s Q4 FY2025 results and the 2026 outlook were somewhat disappointing — with revenue down 5% year over year and a miss on top‑line expectations — profitability remained ahead of forecasts, and the Intermex‑inclusive guidance still points to growth. Management also highlighted early signs of improvement in the previously challenged North American business six weeks into 2026, offering a degree of encouragement despite the softer headline numbers.

As highlighted on the call, the global political environment remains highly volatile, and elections in certain regions can shift corridor dynamics almost overnight. At the same time, Western Union’s global scale provides meaningful balance: when one region slows, another often accelerates.

Their digital‑first strategy and their role as a "gatekeeper"—introducing legacy remittance customers to digital services, often for the first time—strikes me as an effective way to migrate users into higher‑value digital channels. I also view their stablecoin initiative as very promising. Combined with their new stablecards and digital wallets, the strategy makes strong sense to me, especially in markets facing high inflation — as the majority of developing countries currently do. Overall, I continue to see Western Union as an attractive business going forward.

In my view, the CEO provided an excellent summary during the earnings call of how they view their role in the ever‑evolving global money‑transfer marketplace:

People will continue to move in search of opportunity, education, and family, and Western Union will continue to provide trusted, compliant, and accessible financial services. As the market continues to evolve, we continue to see a shift towards the digital channel, particularly among younger and more technologically savvy customer cohorts.

Given that WU is often labeled as a business that’s supposedly "dying,’" I think it’s fitting to conclude with Mark Twain’s famous words: "The reports of my death are greatly exaggerated."

data.title: Why 7.7%-Yielding AMLP Could Pay Even More In The Future
data.description:
The Alerian Master Limited Partnership ETF (AMLP) is one of the best vehicles through which to get exposure to master limited partnerships [MLPs]. MLPs, which have some of the highest yields out of all types of publicly traded securities, are very desirable to income-focused investors, because they offer high and growing income. AMLP yields 7.7%; who wouldn't want a yield like that?

At the same time, individual MLPs can be very risky. Most of them have high dividend yields and high payout ratios, meaning that they usually resort to heavy levels of debt to finance new projects. Given that most MLPs are in the midstream energy space, these projects (e.g. oil pipelines) are usually large and costly. In times of rising interest rates, MLPs’ cash flows can be strained by interest payments and payout ratios can push into unsustainable territory. Here, the risk of dividend cuts becomes very real.

When I call MLP dividend cuts a “risk,” I don’t mean a distant possibility that “could” happen but probably won’t. I mean a true risk that becomes material pretty frequently. From 2016 to 2020, Plains All American Pipeline endured three dividend cuts, which collectively took the quarterly payout from $0.70 to $0.18. Eventually, the payout climbed even higher than its 2016 level, but the unit price never recovered.

When you buy an MLP ETF like AMLP, you spread all of that risk across many holdings, just like you do with stock ETFs. If you have no information edge, that diversification is a benefit in itself: most investors are best off diversifying widely.

In the case of MLPs, the ETF structure comes with yet another benefit: tax simplicity. ETFs are taxed as regular corporations, which eliminates the headache of filing a K-1 tax form, a significant burden to small time investors with no accounting expertise. For this reason, I think an investment in AMLP beats holding individual MLP's for 90% of investors. As I'll show in the ensuing paragraphs, that tax simplicity combined with dividend growth potential makes AMLP a very attractive income investment.

Tax simplicity

MLPs have a notoriously complex tax-structure. Their income passes through to shareholders without being taxed at the company level, resulting in tax savings to the company and, ultimately, to the shareholder. However, these savings come at a major cost: complexity. Because MLPs are technically partnerships, your holdings in them are treated as business ownership. So, you have to file a separate income tax return called a K-1just for your MLPs, much like an entrepreneur must file a business tax return alongside his personal tax return. If you are not an expert accountant, you might need to hire a professional to help you with this, which negates some of the tax efficiency you theoretically enjoy. What’s the difference between $50 in taxes, $50 spent on tax filing software, and $50 paid to an accountant?

AMLP mitigates this problem, in two key ways.

First, when you hold AMLP, you file a 1099 return rather than a K-1 return. The 1099 itself is an additional return on top of your personal income tax return; however, it’s widely thought to be simpler and more user friendly than the K-1. Most people can fill out a form 1099 without expensive tax software or help from an accountant. That added simplicity is a major benefit, as it reduces the time/money you spend filing taxes.

Second, AMLP is taxed as a regular corporation. This means that all of the complexity that stems from holding MLPs is handled by the fund. Once the dividends flow through to you, the shareholder, they have already been taxed. What you are left with is straightforward dividend income, whose tax treatment you are most likely familiar with. So, AMLP delivers a real “double whammy” of tax simplicity, especially compared to other investments in the MLP space.

Portfolio

AMLP’s portfolio is characterized by concentration. With 13 holdings, it is much less diversified than your typical ETF. That’s not really a negative, at least not insofar as MLPs are good assets to be investing in: only a dozen or so large MLPs exist in the world, meaning that AMLP represents its asset class well.

Because of AMLP’s concentration, I am able to review some basic portfolio metrics for all of its holdings. In most articles, I only review the top five or top 10, as looking at all of the holdings in a 500 stock ETF is not viable. I have the space to review all of this fund's holdings. So, without further ado, here are some key value, profitability, growth and dividend metrics for AMLP’s portfolio–all of them courtesy of Seeking Alpha Quant.

Dividend yield
